# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **Stuff To Do With A Loop**
+ **Drawing Graphs Using Loops**
+ **Menu-Driven Programming**
:::

::: notes
Review loop purpose and menu structure before introducing loop-control shorthand and validation patterns.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Increment and Decrement Operators
- Break
- Continue
- Input Validation Strategy
- Input Validation

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_increment_decrement.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_break.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_continue.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_4_input_validation_strategy.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_5_input_validation.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- `++`, `--`, and compound assignment make loop updates easier to read.
- `break` exits the nearest loop or switch immediately.
- `continue` skips the rest of the current loop pass but keeps the loop running.
- Validation strategy decides what a program must check before it trusts input.
- Input validation protects the program boundary before client data is trusted.

<!-- Slide 58 -->

---

# Next Time

- More input validation practice
- Validation helpers
- Validation in menu-driven programs

<!-- Slide 59 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 60 -->

---

## Increment and Decrement Operators: Good Practices

- Use `i++` in ordinary for-loop update expressions.
- Use `+=` or `-=` when the loop moves by more than one.
- Keep increment and decrement statements easy to see.

<!-- Slide 61 -->

---

## Increment and Decrement Operators: Common Mistakes

- Assuming `x++` and `++x` produce the same value inside a larger expression.
- Incrementing the wrong variable in a nested or multi-variable loop.
- Forgetting that `++` always changes the value by exactly one.

<!-- Slide 62 -->

---

## Increment and Decrement Operators: More Examples

```cpp
for (int i = 2; i <= 20; i += 2) {
    cout << i << " ";
}

for (int i = 10; i >= 0; i--) {
    cout << i << " ";
}
```

<!-- Slide 63 -->

---

## Increment and Decrement Operators: Practice Problems

- Rewrite `i = i + 1` using the increment operator.
- Trace `int x = 3; cout << x++ << " " << x;`.
- Write a loop that counts from `100` down to `0` by tens.

<!-- Slide 64 -->

---

## Increment and Decrement Operators: AI Search Terms

- C++ increment decrement operators
- C++ postfix vs prefix increment
- C++ compound assignment operators
- C++ for loop update expression

<!-- Slide 65 -->

---

## Increment and Decrement Operators: Questions for Reflection

- When does shorthand make code easier to read?
- Why does prefix vs postfix not matter in a standalone statement?
- What loop steps cannot be expressed with `++` or `--`?

<!-- Slide 66 -->

---

## Break: Good Practices

- Keep the reason for `break` close to the `break` statement.
- Prefer a clear loop condition when it expresses the exit naturally.
- Use `break` in searches when the answer is already found.

<!-- Slide 67 -->

---

## Break: Common Mistakes

- Assuming one `break` exits every nested loop.
- Confusing `break` in a `switch` with `break` in a loop.
- Using many scattered `break` statements when one loop condition would be clearer.

<!-- Slide 68 -->

---

## Break: More Examples

```cpp
int value;
int count = 0;

while (cin >> value) {
    if (value < 0) {
        break;
    }

    count++;
}
```

<!-- Slide 69 -->

---

## Break: Practice Problems

- Stop reading numbers when the client enters `0`.
- Search a vector and stop at the first negative value.
- Explain what happens when `break` appears inside a `switch` inside a loop.

<!-- Slide 70 -->

---

## Break: AI Search Terms

- C++ break statement loop
- C++ break in switch statement
- C++ sentinel loop break
- C++ stop loop when found

<!-- Slide 71 -->

---

## Break: Questions for Reflection

- Where does execution go immediately after `break`?
- Would the loop be clearer with the stopping rule in the condition?
- What makes a `break` statement easy or hard to trust?

<!-- Slide 72 -->

---

## Continue: Good Practices

- Use `continue` for early skip cases.
- Keep loop-control updates before `continue` in while loops.
- Prefer a plain `if` when it reads more directly.

<!-- Slide 73 -->

---

## Continue: Common Mistakes

- Confusing `continue` with `break`.
- Placing `continue` before the loop-control variable changes.
- Hiding too much logic after multiple `continue` statements.

<!-- Slide 74 -->

---

## Continue: More Examples

```cpp
for (int value : values) {
    if (value == 0) {
        continue;
    }

    cout << 100 / value << endl;
}
```

<!-- Slide 75 -->

---

## Continue: Practice Problems

- Print only odd numbers from `1` to `20` using `continue`.
- Skip blank names in a list of names.
- Rewrite a `continue` example using an `if` statement instead.

<!-- Slide 76 -->

---

## Continue: AI Search Terms

- C++ continue statement loop
- C++ continue vs break
- C++ skip iteration with continue
- C++ continue while loop infinite loop

<!-- Slide 77 -->

---

## Continue: Questions for Reflection

- What code is skipped after `continue`?
- Why can `continue` be risky in a while loop?
- Would an `if` statement be clearer in this case?

<!-- Slide 78 -->

---

## Input Validation Strategy: Good Practices

- Validate from broadest condition to narrowest condition.
- Give a specific error message and reprompt.
- Keep invalid input from reaching the rest of the program.

<!-- Slide 79 -->

---

## Input Validation Strategy: Common Mistakes

- Checking range before checking whether the input is the right type.
- Printing a generic error message when the client needs a specific correction.
- Using a value before the program has decided it is safe.

<!-- Slide 80 -->

---

## Input Validation Strategy: More Examples

```cpp
string name;
cout << "Name: ";
getline(cin, name);

while (name.empty()) {
    cout << "Name cannot be blank. Try again: ";
    getline(cin, name);
}
```

<!-- Slide 81 -->

---

## Input Validation Strategy: Practice Problems

- Validate that a menu choice is between `1` and `4`.
- Validate that a password is not blank and has at least seven characters.
- Validate that a percent is between `0` and `100`.

<!-- Slide 82 -->

---

## Input Validation Strategy: AI Search Terms

- C++ input validation strategy
- C++ getline empty string validation
- C++ range validation loop
- C++ allow by default deny by default validation

<!-- Slide 83 -->

---

## Input Validation Strategy: Questions for Reflection

- What is the first thing that could be wrong with this input?
- What message would help the client fix the mistake?
- Where does the program first trust the data?

<!-- Slide 84 -->

---

## Input Validation: Good Practices

- Validate immediately after input.
- Write the loop condition in terms of invalid data.
- Give an error message that names the valid range or choices.

<!-- Slide 85 -->

---

## Input Validation: Common Mistakes

- Processing the value before validation finishes.
- Forgetting to read a replacement value inside the loop.
- Treating type failure and range failure as the same problem.

<!-- Slide 86 -->

---

## Input Validation: More Examples

```cpp
int month;

cout << "Month (1-12): ";
cin >> month;

while (month < 1 || month > 12) {
    cout << "Month must be 1 through 12: ";
    cin >> month;
}
```

<!-- Slide 87 -->

---

## Input Validation: Practice Problems

- Validate a quantity from `1` to `99`.
- Validate a menu choice of `A`, `D`, or `Q`.
- Explain why `score < 0 && score > 100` is the wrong invalid condition.

<!-- Slide 88 -->

---

## Input Validation: AI Search Terms

- C++ input validation while loop
- C++ validate integer range
- C++ cin fail clear ignore
- C++ input validation menu choice

<!-- Slide 89 -->

---

## Input Validation: Questions for Reflection

- What does the program know after the validation loop exits?
- What error message would help the client fix the input?
- Where should validation happen in a larger program?

<!-- Slide 90 -->
